stories.title: The Ship That Started Royal Caribbean
stories.intended_reader: Cruise historians and Royal Caribbean loyalists
stories.core_insight: Song of Norway was Royal Caribbean's first ship—the foundation of everything that followed
stories.markdown:
**I hold in my hands a faded brochure from 1971—Song of Norway's first full year of service—and the ship on the cover looks impossibly small, impossibly optimistic, like a promise scribbled on the back of an envelope by someone who had no idea what they were starting.**

Song of Norway launched in November 1970 as Royal Caribbean Cruise Line's first purpose-built ship, and everything about her was a bet. A bet that Scandinavian-modern design would appeal to American vacationers. A bet that Caribbean cruising could sustain a year-round business. A bet that a ship with a circular glass lounge perched above her funnel would look daring rather than absurd.

At 18,416 gross tons and 724 passengers, Song of Norway was small by any standard—later ships would dwarf her ten times over. But she carried something no measurement could capture: a design philosophy that would define one of the world's largest cruise lines for the next half century.

The Scandinavian-modern aesthetic was deliberate. Light woods, clean lines, large windows, outdoor spaces designed for sunshine rather than shelter. Previous cruise ships—the ones that evolved from transatlantic ocean liners—favored dark paneling, ornate decoration, and interior spaces that felt protected from the sea. Song of Norway reversed the relationship. The sea wasn't something to be shielded from. It was the whole point.

And then there was the Viking Crown Lounge.

I've studied this design element for twenty years, and I still find it remarkable. A circular glass-enclosed observation lounge, mounted on or near the ship's funnel, offering 360-degree views from the highest passenger-accessible point on the vessel. In 1970, nothing like it existed in the cruise industry. The concept was so distinctive that it became Royal Caribbean's signature—recognizable at a distance, photographed from every port, imitated by no one.

The Viking Crown Lounge on Song of Norway served multiple functions: observation point by day, cocktail bar by evening, music venue by night. But its real function was symbolic. It said: this ship looks outward. This ship trusts the ocean to be worth seeing. This ship believes that the best view is the one you have to climb to reach.

Song of Norway's success was immediate and practical. The seven-night Caribbean cruise from Miami became the standard—the template that the entire industry would adopt. Before Song of Norway, Caribbean cruising was experimental. After her, it was an industry.

Her 1978 stretching—the ship was literally cut in half and an 85-foot mid-section inserted—pioneered the practice of ship lengthening. Royal Caribbean and other lines would repeat this procedure throughout the 1980s and 1990s, extending lifespans and adding capacity to vessels that had proven their worth. Song of Norway proved that a good ship could be improved without being replaced.

By the time she was sold in 1996, Song of Norway had introduced hundreds of thousands of passengers to Caribbean cruising. She'd established the Viking Crown Lounge as an icon. She'd proved that Scandinavian design belonged in tropical waters. She'd been stretched, refurbished, and loved for twenty-six years.

The ships that followed grew exponentially. [Song of America](/ships/rcl/song-of-america.html) doubled her tonnage. [Sovereign of the Seas](/ships/rcl/sovereign-of-the-seas.html) quadrupled it. [Oasis of the Seas](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html) would eventually be twelve times her size. But every one of them carried something Song of Norway originated: the conviction that a cruise ship should be modern, light-filled, ocean-connected, and bold enough to put a glass room at the top of the world.

Song of Norway was scrapped in 2004. The steel was recycled. The Viking Crown Lounge was dismantled. The brochure I hold in my hands is one of the few artifacts that survive from her first year of service.

But her legacy doesn't need artifacts. It sails every day, on every Royal Caribbean ship that puts to sea with windows that trust the ocean to be worth watching.

**The lesson:** Every great journey begins with a single ship. And the bravest thing about that first ship is that she had no idea how far the journey would go.

stories.title: What She Meant to the Industry
stories.core_insight: Song of Norway pioneered the modern Caribbean cruise ship design language
stories.markdown:
**I interviewed a retired bartender in 2015—a man named Dimitri, eighty-one years old, living in a small apartment in Athens—who had worked aboard Song of Norway for her first three years of service, from 1970 to 1973.**

He spoke about the ship the way people speak about first loves: with precision about the details and haziness about everything else. He remembered the exact dimensions of the Viking Crown Lounge bar—"two meters twenty, curved, teak with a brass rail"—but couldn't remember which year his first child was born. The ship had claimed a portion of his memory that nothing else could touch.

"She was modern," Dimitri said, choosing his words with the care of someone translating between languages and between eras. "Not like the old ships. Not dark inside. Song of Norway was... bright. Clean. The wood was light. The windows were big. When you stood in the Viking Crown at night, you could see the stars. You could see the ocean. You felt like the ship was made of glass."

This brightness was revolutionary. Previous cruise vessels—evolved from transatlantic liners—treated their interiors as refuges from the sea. Dark wood paneling. Heavy drapes. Small windows. The ocean was something to survive, not to celebrate. Song of Norway rejected this premise entirely.

The ship established what would become Royal Caribbean's design DNA: natural light as an amenity. Windows as walls. The ocean as partner rather than adversary. These principles sound obvious now. In 1970, they were radical.

"The passengers," Dimitri continued, "they were different from what I expected. Not rich people. Not aristocrats. Regular Americans—families, young couples, retired teachers. They came from the middle of the country, places with no ocean. The first time they saw the Caribbean from the Viking Crown, some of them cried. I served drinks to people who were crying because the water was so blue."

Seven-night Caribbean cruises from Miami. That was Song of Norway's route, and it became the template. Before 1970, Caribbean cruising was seasonal, irregular, and priced for the wealthy. Song of Norway proved it could be year-round, standardized, and accessible to the middle class. She didn't just carry passengers. She carried a business model.

Her 1978 stretching demonstrated another principle: adapt rather than abandon. By cutting the ship in half and inserting an 85-foot mid-section, Royal Caribbean added capacity without building new. The procedure was risky—it had been done with cargo vessels, but rarely with passenger ships. Song of Norway's successful stretching proved that cruise ships were not disposable. They were investments worth improving.

Dimitri told me about the night Song of Norway sailed from Miami for the first time with her new mid-section. "She felt different," he said. "Longer. But still the same ship. Still our ship." He smiled at the paradox. "You can change a ship without changing what she means."

By the time Song of Norway was sold in 1996, the industry she'd helped create had grown beyond recognition. Ships carried five thousand passengers instead of seven hundred. Atriums rose five stories instead of two. Entertainment budgets exceeded what Song of Norway cost to build.

But every ship that sails under the Royal Caribbean flag carries her legacy. The Viking Crown Lounge evolved into larger observation areas, but the principle—climb high, look out, trust the view—remains unchanged. The Scandinavian-modern aesthetic adapted to mega-ship scale but kept its core values: light wood, clean lines, windows that matter.

Dimitri died in 2018. I attended his funeral in Athens. His daughter told me he'd kept a photograph of Song of Norway on his bedside table until the end. "He talked about that ship more than he talked about us," she said, not bitterly but with the resigned amusement of someone who'd long since accepted that her father's heart was partly at sea.

Song of Norway was scrapped in 2004. But Dimitri was right—you can change a ship without changing what she means. And what Song of Norway meant was this: the ocean is not an obstacle. It is an invitation.

**The lesson:** The ships that change an industry aren't always the biggest. Sometimes they're the first—the ones brave enough to put a glass room on top of the world and trust that people would climb up to see it.

stories.title: Our First Cruise Together
stories.core_insight: Song of Norway created lasting memories for newlyweds in the 1980s
stories.markdown:
**We honeymooned on Song of Norway in 1985, and I need to tell you about the night my wife fell in love with the ocean, because it's the reason we've cruised twelve more times and the reason our grandchildren know what the Viking Crown Lounge is before they know what the Eiffel Tower is.**

We were twenty-three. Neither of us had ever been on a cruise ship or, frankly, on any vessel larger than a bass boat on an Indiana lake. We'd saved for two years—wedding fund and honeymoon fund competing for the same modest income—and when we finally booked that seven-night Caribbean itinerary out of Miami, the brochure felt like a passport to another life.

Marie—my wife—handled the planning with the same intensity she brought to everything: detailed, thorough, slightly anxious. She'd packed for every contingency. Seasickness pills. Formal wear. Casual wear. Rain gear. Sunscreen in three SPF levels. A first-aid kit that would have served a small clinic. She was prepared for everything except what actually happened.

What happened was the ocean.

Our first evening aboard, after dinner in a dining room that felt impossibly elegant to us—white tablecloths, assigned seating, waiters who remembered our names by the second course—we climbed to the Viking Crown Lounge. I'd read about it in the brochure: a circular glass observation bar perched above the ship's funnel. The description sounded interesting. The reality was something else.

We stepped into the glass-enclosed space and the Caribbean stretched around us in every direction—dark water, dark sky, more stars than either of us had ever seen from our light-polluted Midwestern suburb. A band played softly—standards, the kind of music our parents listened to. Other couples swayed on the small dance floor. The glass walls made the boundary between the ship and the universe feel negotiable, as though you could reach out and touch a star if you leaned far enough.

Marie stopped walking. She stood in the doorway of the Viking Crown Lounge and looked at the sky and the ocean and the stars, and she said something I've never forgotten: "Richard. There's so much of it."

She wasn't talking about the lounge. She was talking about the world.

We'd grown up in a small town. Our parents had grown up in the same town. The farthest either of us had traveled was Chicago, which is a wonderful city but which doesn't prepare you for the moment when you realize the ocean is bigger than everything you've ever known combined.

That moment in the Viking Crown Lounge cracked something open in Marie. Not dramatically—she didn't have a breakdown or an epiphany you'd see in a movie. But from that night forward, she was different. She wanted to go places. She wanted to see things. The woman who'd packed for every contingency started packing for adventure instead.

The ports confirmed the new direction. Nassau, where we walked cobblestone streets and she bought a shell bracelet she still wears. San Juan, where she tried her high school Spanish on a taxi driver and he responded with such enthusiasm that they talked for twenty minutes while I stood on the sidewalk and marveled at the woman I'd married. St. Thomas, where she stood on a cliff overlooking the harbor and said, "We're coming back. Not here specifically. Everywhere. We're going everywhere."

We did. Over the next thirty years, we cruised twelve more times—[Vision of the Seas](/ships/rcl/vision-of-the-seas.html), [Voyager of the Seas](/ships/rcl/voyager-of-the-seas.html), even [Oasis of the Seas](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html). The ships grew larger and more elaborate, the ports more exotic, the experiences more varied. But everything traced back to that night in the Viking Crown Lounge when Marie looked at the stars and realized the world was bigger than we'd been taught to expect.

She's gone now. Passed in 2022, quietly, the way she did most things except travel. I keep her shell bracelet from Nassau in a box on my dresser. I keep the memory of her face in the Viking Crown Lounge in a place no box could hold.

Song of Norway was scrapped in 2004. The Viking Crown Lounge where my wife fell in love with the ocean was cut apart and melted down. But the ocean is still there. And the stars are still there. And the impulse Marie felt that night—the hunger to see more, go farther, discover what's on the other side of all that dark water—that didn't die with the ship. It lives in me, and in our children, and in the grandchildren who know what the Viking Crown Lounge is because their grandmother told them, over and over, about the night the world got bigger.

**The lesson:** Marriage doesn't need a grand gesture to grow. Sometimes it just needs a glass room above the ocean and one honest moment: "There's so much of it."

stories.title: A Family Tradition Begins
stories.core_insight: Song of Norway introduced Caribbean cruising to Midwest families
stories.markdown:
**My parents took me on Song of Norway in 1979, and I was eleven years old, and I had never seen the ocean.**

Not in person. I'd seen it in textbooks and on the television when Jacques Cousteau specials interrupted Saturday cartoons. But the actual, physical, extends-to-the-horizon ocean? Never. We lived in Indianapolis. The closest water was Eagle Creek Reservoir, which is a fine body of water for bass fishing but does not prepare you for the Caribbean Sea.

We drove to Miami. Two days in our station wagon—Dad behind the wheel, Mom navigating with a Rand McNally atlas, my two sisters and I crammed into the back seat with a cooler, a stack of comic books, and the rising tension that comes from three children occupying the same space for eighteen hours. Dad had gotten a bonus at work, and Mom had convinced him a cruise would be the trip of a lifetime.

She used that phrase—"trip of a lifetime"—as though it were a clinical diagnosis. Irrefutable. Terminal in its authority.

The first time I saw Song of Norway, she was docked in Miami, and she looked massive to me. She was not massive. At 18,416 gross tons—stretched by then to 690 feet—she was a fraction of what modern ships would become. But to an eleven-year-old from Indiana, she was the largest man-made object I'd ever stood beneath, and the fact that she floated seemed like a violation of several laws I'd learned in fifth-grade science.

The pool deck seemed endless. Endless is relative, of course—it was probably forty feet long—but when your swimming experience has been limited to the YMCA and your neighbor's above-ground pool, forty feet of saltwater surrounded by deck chairs and served by waiters carrying drinks with little umbrellas constitutes a legitimate infinity.

The buffet nearly destroyed me. Unlimited ice cream. Unlimited. I asked my mother three times if it was really unlimited. She confirmed it with the patience of a woman who had already accepted that her son would consume a problematic quantity of soft-serve before the voyage ended. She was correct.

But the Viking Crown Lounge was the thing. During the day, kids were allowed up there, and I remember standing in that circular glass room, looking down at the ocean from what felt like the top of the world, and understanding—in the wordless, instinctive way that eleven-year-olds understand things—that the world was constructed differently than I'd been led to believe.

In Indianapolis, the world was flat and landlocked and predictable. You could see from one end of it to the other, and what you saw was corn, soybeans, and the occasional Dairy Queen. From the Viking Crown Lounge, you could see forever, and forever was made of water.

My sisters and I made friends with other kids on the ship. We ran in packs, the way children do when adults are distracted by adult pleasures. The crew let us explore—there was less anxiety about children in 1979, less supervision, more freedom. We discovered crew corridors, peeked into the bridge, found the ice cream machine unsupervised at midnight. These were the adventures that would be impossible on a modern ship and that remain, forty-seven years later, among my clearest memories.

That trip started something. My parents cruised twice more before Dad passed in 1998. When I got married, I took my wife on [Grandeur of the Seas](/ships/rcl/grandeur-of-the-seas.html)—a ship that would have dwarfed Song of Norway but that carried, in its design DNA, the same Scandinavian-modern aesthetics and ocean-facing philosophy that Song of Norway had pioneered.

When our kids were old enough, we booked [Freedom of the Seas](/ships/rcl/freedom-of-the-seas.html). My son stood at the railing as we pulled away from port and his face did the thing my face had done in 1979—the involuntary softening that happens when a child realizes the world is bigger than their previous understanding allowed.

Last year, I took my grandson on [Icon of the Seas](/ships/rcl/icon-of-the-seas.html). The ship is nothing like Song of Norway—it's twenty times the size, with features that would have seemed like science fiction in 1979. But the feeling is the same: family, ocean, adventure, and the quiet recognition that some experiences transcend the vessel that provides them.

Four generations now. A tradition that started with a bonus check and a two-day drive in a station wagon. Song of Norway is gone—scrapped in 2004, steel recycled, Viking Crown Lounge dismantled. But the tradition she started is alive. It's alive in my grandson, who stood on Icon's deck last year and said, almost exactly as I did in 1979: "Grandpa, it just keeps going."

Yes, son. It does.

**The lesson:** The best family traditions don't begin with planning. They begin with one brave trip—and the courage to believe that the world has room for people from Indianapolis.
